display_name: IS-IS adjacency failure on a point-to-point link may be a network type mismatch — one side P2P, the other broadcast
context: IS-IS adjacency does not form on a point-to-point link (physical or logical) between two routers; both sides show IS-IS enabled on the interface but the adjacency stays down or in Init state
observation: IS-IS adjacency will not come up on a link; one side may show the neighbor in Init state, the other may show no neighbor at all; IS-IS hello packets are being sent but the adjacency never progresses to Up
naive_interpretation: There is a Layer 2 or Layer 3 connectivity issue on the link, or IS-IS authentication is misconfigured, or area/level mismatches are preventing adjacency formation
correct_interpretation: IS-IS supports two network types: broadcast (LAN) and point-to-point. On broadcast interfaces, IS-IS elects a Designated Intermediate System (DIS) and forms adjacencies using 3-way LAN hellos. On point-to-point interfaces, IS-IS uses a different hello format (P2P IIH) with a 3-way handshake defined in RFC 5303. If one side of a link is configured as point-to-point and the other as broadcast, the hello packets are incompatible — each side sends its network-type-specific hello and silently discards the other side's hellos because they are a different PDU type. The adjacency never forms, and there is typically no error log or explicit rejection message. This is a silent failure. The fix is to ensure both sides use the same network type. On physical point-to-point links (serial, P2P Ethernet), both sides should use 'isis network point-to-point' (or the equivalent). The mismatch is especially common when one side is a Cisco device (which defaults to broadcast on Ethernet) and the other is Junos (which defaults to point-to-point on many interface types).
why: IS-IS P2P hellos (IIH type 20) and LAN hellos (IIH type 15/16) are different PDU types at the IS-IS protocol level — a router expecting one type silently drops the other

Unlike OSPF, which logs a network type mismatch explicitly, IS-IS does not generate a clear error message when the hello types don't match — the adjacency simply stays in Init or Down with no diagnostic output

Ethernet interfaces default to broadcast network type on most Cisco platforms but default to point-to-point on Junos and some SONiC configurations — cross-vendor links are the most common scenario for this mismatch
The RFC 5303 three-way handshake (P2P adjacency) is carried inside P2P IIH packets only — a broadcast-side router never processes this TLV because it never parses the P2P IIH

confirmation_commands.purpose: Display the IS-IS network type (point-to-point or broadcast/LAN) configured on the local interface — compare with the remote side
Check the IS-IS circuit type and network type to verify it matches the remote end
Display IS-IS interface parameters including network type (point-to-point vs LAN)
Verify the IS-IS interface type (point-to-point or LAN) and check for adjacency state
Display adjacency state — an adjacency stuck in Init with no error typically indicates a hello type mismatch

traps: Debugging IS-IS hellos and seeing packets sent but no adjacency forming — the hellos ARE being sent, they are just the wrong PDU type for the other side to process

Checking authentication, area IDs, and MTU (all valid troubleshooting steps) while missing the network type mismatch — this is especially frustrating because all other parameters can be correct and the adjacency still won't form
Assuming that because both sides show 'IS-IS enabled' on the interface, the configuration is compatible — the network type is a separate parameter from IS-IS enablement
Not checking the remote side's configuration — the mismatch is only visible when you compare both sides; each side's local config looks valid in isolation
On multi-vendor links, assuming both sides have the same default network type for Ethernet — Cisco defaults to broadcast, Junos often defaults to P2P

sources.title: RFC 5303 - Three-Way Handshake for IS-IS Point-to-Point Adjacencies
